title: Time as the Anchor: Time Series Basics
concept: A time series is a sequence of data points recorded at regular intervals over time, where chronological order is essential because the future depends on the past.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a decision stump?

options.text: A decision tree with a max depth of exactly 1, used as a weak learner.
A tree that has had its root node deleted.
A model that aggregates predictions in parallel.

exercises.explain: Correct! A decision stump is the simplest tree model, splitting on only one question.

cards.title: The movie script vs. the stack of index cards
cards.body: If you shuffle the pages of a movie script, the story is ruined because the plot is a sequence where step 2 depends on step 1. But if you have a stack of cards showing house prices in a city, shuffling them doesn't change the facts. Time series data is like that script — the order of the rows is the most important part.

cards.title: Time Series Forecasting
cards.body: A time series is data collected sequentially over time at set intervals (e.g. daily, hourly). Forecasting is using this historical timeline to predict future values. Because the future depends on the past (temporal dependency), we cannot shuffle or randomize the rows.

cards.title: Walking in the snow
cards.body: If you look at footprints in the snow, your next step is highly constrained by where you are standing right now. You can't teleport to the other side of the yard. Similarly, today's temperature or stock price is heavily influenced by yesterday's value — it doesn't just jump randomly to a new scale.

cards.title: Everyday timelines
cards.body: Examples of time series include: 1. Daily electricity consumption of a city, 2. Monthly store sales, 3. Hourly heart rate tracking. In contrast, predicting a person's weight based on their height is standard regression — time is not the anchor of the prediction.

cards.title: Time series isn't regular regression
cards.body: You might think: 'Can't we just treat time like a regular feature column and run linear regression?' In time series, data points close in time are highly correlated (autocorrelation). This breaks standard regression assumptions, requiring models specifically designed to handle time dependency.

left.text:
Rows are independent.
Shuffling has no effect.
e.g., list of houses for sale.

right.text:
Rows are linked sequentially.
Order is critical.
e.g., hourly temperature logs.

exercises.prompt: What is the key defining feature of time series data?

options.text: The rows are recorded sequentially over time, and their chronological order is critical.
The data is completely independent and can be shuffled without loss of meaning.
The target column must only contain text categories.
The data is collected using unsupervised clustering algorithms.

exercises.explain: Correct! Chronological ordering and sequential dependency are the defining traits of time series.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You can shuffle the rows of a daily sales dataset before building a time series model without causing any problems.
exercises.explain: False! Shuffling breaks the chronological sequence, destroying the model's ability to learn how the past predicts the future.

exercises.sentence: In time series data, when observations close together in time are highly correlated, this is called ___.
exercises.explain: Autocorrelation measures how much a data point relates to its own past values.

exercises.prompt: Which of these datasets is a time series?

options.text: Hourly temperature readings recorded at a weather station over a year.
A list of 500 patient weights, heights, and eye colors.
A collection of customer review texts sorted alphabetically.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sequentially logged hourly temperature readings are a classic time series.

exercises.prompt: You are asked to forecast next week's ice cream sales for a beach shop. Why does standard linear regression struggle if we just use 'Day number' as a feature, and what does a time series model do instead?

options.text: Linear regression assumes sales on Tuesday are independent of Monday's sales. A time series model leverages temporal dependency, using recent history directly to predict the next step.
Linear regression can only predict categories, not sales numbers.
A time series model ignores past history and assumes sales are constant.
Time series models require the data to be clustered first.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Standard regression ignores the fact that consecutive days are linked. A time series model embraces this dependency, using past patterns directly. Next, let's look at the first major pattern time series models look for: Trends!
